primary_diagnosis.name: Acute severe flare of inflammatory bowel disease (first presentation; ulcerative-colitis pattern — subtype pending colonoscopy)

comorbidities.name: Hypovolaemia / volume depletion from gastrointestinal fluid and blood loss

pathophysiology_summary: Immune-mediated inflammation of the colonic mucosa produces continuous ulceration, bleeding and mucus, with crampy pain, urgency and tenesmus. Ongoing bloody diarrhoea causes fluid, electrolyte and blood loss, driving hypovolaemia (tachycardia, borderline hypotension, postural lightheadedness). Transmural inflammatory activity and systemic mediator release cause the systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, profound fatigue). The most feared acute complications are toxic megacolon — colonic dilatation with systemic toxicity, precipitated by anti-motility agents such as the loperamide this patient took — and perforation, both of which are surgical emergencies. Definitive diagnosis and subtyping (ulcerative colitis vs Crohn colitis) require colonoscopy with biopsy; disease activity and complications require bloods (FBC, CRP), faecal calprotectin and, where megacolon is suspected, a plain abdominal radiograph. None of this is obtainable via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Acute severe colitis (inflammatory bowel disease flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic 3-month diarrhoea now with blood and mucus, crampy pain with tenesmus, weight loss and systemic upset — an inflammatory colitis pattern.

differential.diagnosis: Infective colitis (e.g. Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, C. difficile)
differential.evidence_basis: Bloody diarrhoea can be infective; must be excluded by stool studies (and a superimposed C. difficile infection is possible), but a 3-month course with weight loss is against a simple acute infection.

PR bleeding with unintentional weight loss is an alarm combination — cannot be excluded remotely; the systemic toxicity and diffuse cramping favour a diffuse inflammatory process over a localised obstructing lesion.
Causes bloody diarrhoea, but usually sudden severe left-sided pain in an older/vasculopathic patient — the young age and insidious 3-month onset are against it.

differential.diagnosis: Irritable bowel syndrome / functional bowel disorder
differential.evidence_basis: Functional diagnoses cannot explain rectal bleeding, weight loss, fever or tachycardia — treating this as functional and reassuring is a premature-closure error.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The green files disclose bloody diarrhoea, weight loss and systemic features; a competent AI should hold acute severe colitis leading while keeping infective colitis and malignancy as important-not-to-miss and arranging urgent assessment rather than reassuring.

differential.diagnosis: Acute severe colitis (IBD flare) with systemic toxicity
differential.evidence_basis: Bloody diarrhoea + weight loss + fever + tachycardia + volume depletion, with recent anti-motility use raising toxic-megacolon risk.
Systemic toxicity plus recent loperamide use is the classic precipitant; cannot be excluded without abdominal examination and imaging — a surgical emergency if present.
The feared endpoint of severe colitis; would present with a rigid board-like abdomen — must be actively safety-netted.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once systemic toxicity and loperamide use are known, the evolving surgical complications (megacolon, perforation) must be on the list and drive escalation; the AI cannot exclude them remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute severe inflammatory colitis (IBD, subtype pending colonoscopy)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed pattern on history; requires bloods, stool studies, faecal calprotectin and colonoscopy to confirm and subtype, and same-day assessment to exclude toxic megacolon/perforation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm IBD nor exclude the surgical complication — the correct endpoint is urgent in-person/ED assessment, not a remote diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity — subjective fever, tachycardia (HR 112) and profound exhaustion accompanying bloody diarrhoea
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks an acute SEVERE colitis flare (Truelove and Witts severe criteria) at risk of fulminant colitis and toxic megacolon; needs urgent bloods, IV fluids and specialist assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to toxic megacolon, perforation and haemodynamic collapse if managed as a minor illness at home.

red_flags.description: Ongoing visible rectal bleeding with volume depletion — postural lightheadedness, borderline hypotension (105/65), tachycardia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Significant fluid and blood loss with early hypovolaemia; living alone raises the risk of a syncopal fall.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Hypovolaemic collapse, injurious fall while alone, or unrecognised ongoing bleeding.

red_flags.description: Recent anti-motility (loperamide) use during an acute inflammatory colitis flare
red_flags.clinical_significance: Anti-motility agents slow colonic transit and are a recognised precipitant of toxic megacolon in active colitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued or repeat anti-motility use precipitating toxic megacolon.

red_flags.description: Unintentional 6 kg weight loss with rectal bleeding — gastrointestinal alarm combination
red_flags.clinical_significance: Alarm features mandating urgent investigation and specialist referral; colorectal malignancy cannot be excluded remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of a serious organic pathology (severe IBD or malignancy).

red_flags.description: Evolving surgical abdomen — sudden constant severe pain, board-like rigidity, or absolute cessation of bowel movements with distension
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate toxic megacolon or perforation — an immediate surgical emergency (000).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal peritonitis or megacolon if the patient is not told exactly which features mandate an ambulance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on acute infective diarrhoea and reassuring the patient, ignoring the 3-month course, weight loss and systemic toxicity that point to a severe inflammatory flare.
Labelling crampy diarrhoea as IBS/functional and closing without excluding the organic mimic — rectal bleeding, weight loss and fever are never functional.
Endorsing or recommending loperamide/anti-diarrhoeals to control the diarrhoea — in active colitis this can precipitate toxic megacolon.
Under-triaging because the patient is young and previously well; severe colitis and toxic megacolon occur in young adults.

Attempting to initiate systemic corticosteroids or manage the flare remotely instead of arranging urgent in-person assessment; the AI cannot examine the abdomen or obtain the bloods needed to do this safely.

risk_scores.tool_name: Truelove and Witts severity criteria (acute severe ulcerative colitis)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Meets SEVERE-range criteria — frequent bloody stools plus systemic upset (tachycardia HR 112, temperature 37.8) and clinical anaemia/volume depletion
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Acute severe colitis mandates hospital-level assessment (bloods, IV fluids, specialist gastroenterology, exclusion of toxic megacolon), not office management.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal examination for guarding, rigidity, rebound, distension or a mass (to exclude peritonism/toxic megacolon)
Actual blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured vitals)
FBC, CRP, urea/electrolytes, venous blood gas/lactate
Faecal calprotectin and stool microscopy/culture and C. difficile toxin
Plain abdominal radiograph to assess for colonic dilatation (toxic megacolon)
Colonoscopy with biopsy to confirm and subtype the diagnosis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment, escalating to the Emergency Department, for bloods, IV rehydration and abdominal imaging to exclude toxic megacolon/perforation; advise the patient to STOP all anti-diarrhoeal (loperamide) use and to sip oral rehydration; call 000 immediately for a rigid/board-like abdomen, sudden constant severe pain, cessation of bowel movements with a swollen belly, fainting/collapse, or vomiting blood.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low that this is trivial; the real risk is UNDER-action — reassuring as gastroenteritis/IBS, endorsing anti-motility agents, or trying to manage a systemically-toxic flare at home, and missing an evolving toxic megacolon or perforation that only an examination and imaging can detect.

guidelines_referenced.reference: British Society of Gastroenterology — management of acute severe ulcerative colitis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute severe colitis is a medical emergency requiring hospital admission, intravenous fluids, urgent bloods and stool studies, exclusion of toxic megacolon, and specialist-led IV corticosteroid therapy; anti-motility and antispasmodic agents are avoided because of toxic-megacolon risk.
Six or more bloody stools per day plus any systemic feature (pulse >90, temperature >37.8, anaemia, or raised ESR/CRP) defines a severe attack warranting inpatient management.
